records.ety: [OE. silver, selver, seolver, AS. seolfor, siolfur, siolufr, silofr, sylofr; akin to OS. silubar, OFries. selover, D. zilver, LG. sulver, OHG. silabar, silbar, G. silber, Icel. silfr, Sw. silfver, Dan. sölv, Goth. silubr, Russ. serebro, Lith. sidabras; of unknown origin.]

defs: A soft white metallic element, sonorous, ductile, very malleable, and capable of a high degree of polish. It is found native, and also combined with sulphur, arsenic, antimony, chlorine, etc., in the minerals argentite, proustite, pyrargyrite, ceragyrite, etc. Silver is one of the “noble” metals, so-called, not being easily oxidized, and is used for coin, jewelry, plate, and a great variety of articles. Symbol Ag (Argentum). Atomic weight 107.7. Specific gravity 10.5.
notes: ☞ Silver was known under the name of luna to the ancients and also to the alchemists. Some of its compounds, as the halogen salts, are remarkable for the effect of light upon them, and are used in photography.

defs: Coin made of silver; silver money.
Anything having the luster or appearance of silver.

notes: ☞ Silver is used in the formation of many compounds of obvious meaning; as, silver-armed, silver-bright, silver-buskined, silver-coated, silver-footed, silver-haired, silver-headed, silver-mantled, silver-plated, silver-slippered, silver-sounding, silver-studded, silver-tongued, silver-white. See Silver, a.

defs: Of or pertaining to silver; made of silver; as, silver leaf; a silver cup.
Giving a clear, ringing sound soft and clear.
quotes: Others, on silver lakes and rivers, bathed Their downy breast.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Silvered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Silvering.]

defs: To cover with silver; to give a silvery appearance to by applying a metal of a silvery color; as, to silver a pin; to silver a glass mirror plate with an amalgam of tin and mercury.
To polish like silver; to impart a brightness to, like that of silver.
quotes: And smiling calmness silvered o'er the deep.

defs: To make hoary, or white, like silver.
quotes: His head was silvered o'er with age.
The eastern sky began to silver and shine.
